text: Abstract: The rule of law in an illiberal context The 2010 Fidesz victory and the populist radical right (PRR) repositioning of the Hungarian state has triggered dramatic changes to domestic state structures, contributing to Hungary’s drift into democratic backsliding and a growing clash with the EU. All these developments culminated in the European Commission decision to impose conditionality on Hungary in April 2022. This article takes a cross-sectoral bargaining approach to analyse the linkages between EU internal conflicts around democratic backsliding and rule of law issues on the one hand, and foreign policy on the other. The EU-Ukraine support package, which was part of the broader responses to the Russian invasion of Ukraine, exemplified such linkages. While bargaining in the context of EU Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) is often considered to take place in an ‘insulated setting’, our analysis demonstrates that foreign policy is increasingly being used as a political currency in intra-EU negotiations. This points towards the populist ‘domestication’ of foreign policy.
